speaker: interviewer
text: This is the interview with Dr. Helen Fagin. Today is February 11, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to introduce my family. My wonderful husband, Sidney Fagin, without whose support, encouragement, and love I could not have accomplished anything after my survival from the Holocaust. My daughter, Judith. Judith Eve, who is one of the brightest, most intelligent people I know. Who is a very artistic and creative person. She studied art history. She studied architecture. And now she is a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] person in development of very large projects throughout this country. Very capable, very bright, and very wonderful young woman. My son, Gary, Gary S. Fagin, whose creativity brought him through a number of fine universities, ending up with a musical career from Yale University. He is a conductor. He's a cellist. He's a composer. He's an arranger. And he is a jack of all trades at the highest degree when it comes to, truly, music appreciation and creativity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something about Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I think what I can say about my dear wife, Helen, is the impact and the-- that she has had on thousands and thousands of people. And I, I would like to quote something to you. There was a time-- which would be indicative of what people think about her and how she influences people around her. There was a commencement at the College of Science--

speaker: interviewee
text: Arts and Sciences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arts and Sciences at the University of Miami. And Helen was a-- Dr. Fagin was the keynote speaker. And I remember the Dean introduced her to the students, I now introduce you as-- to Dr. Helen Fagin, the conscience of the university. Now I think this is an accolade that is just unbelievable. I remember that. She says it was never said, but believe me.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was said, I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it very clearly. And this is indicative of, of, of her. And another thing, the Judaic background. There was a time, I think it was the first time, that Passover came and Judy and Gary were at college. And there was nobody home. And we have never had that before. So Helen decided to invite some of the students for Passover. Now I want you to know that this was the time of Vietnam, dirty feet, torn clothes, long hair, anti-establishment, and so on. Table was set beautifully. And I remember she invited six, or eight, or ten students.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: How about 13? That's her memory. I don't have it. Anyway, we were all ready, and I had-- I had my shirt on. And I didn't know whether to put a tie on. And I said, do you remember telling the kids what to wear, remembering the times. And she said, no. Now keep in mind that every Monday night, we had open house. Many students would come. You may-- she may have told you about that. I looked out the window and I wondered, what are these kids going to wear when they come to Passover beautifully set up with the candles and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked out in-- the window, and a car drove up. This was in Miami, Florida. And one young man got out. And he was dressed as though he walked out of the middle of a men's magazine. It took seconds for me to run back, put a shirt on, put-- change my shirt, put a tie on, and a jacket, and come to the corner door. And he came up to the door and said, Mr. Fagin, can we bring a gift for Dr. Fagin? I said, you bring anything you want. I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to go back a moment, because when I asked her did she tell the students about-- very nonchalantly, she said, I don't want-- they know what to dress when they come to my house. And this is indicative of the respect that they had for her. And the other thing about influence, which we always tell to many people. She kept getting letters-- and we still do-- from students. And one time, we got a letter from a student in Chicago who was an attorney. And it seems she wrote in this letter, which she probably still has, that she had a moral dilemma. A major problem. And she came home and didn't know how to handle this problem. And she said, and I quote, 'and Dr. Fagin, I took you out of my hip pocket and I knew what the right answer was.' I can tell you all kind of stories like that. And this is so indicative of her influence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, sweetheart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's very modest about these things. There's a gentleman here by name of Kurt Baum. And he coined a phrase for her that I love. He says, your wife is a QVA, a quiet voice of authority. And that's, that's-- this is something. But this is what I know that she has done. Her influence around her, the students and so on, is just immense. And this is a legacy that she leaves behind her. All comes from wonderful background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss with your children your Holocaust experience when they were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. They, they knew what I went through, but I didn't discuss it. Now they have the document that I wrote, so they would know the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like them to comment on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother introduced Gary and I by our full names. And what she didn't say is that we're both named after our grandparents, her parents. And these are grandparents that, of course, we never met and knew almost nothing about. And it was only two weeks ago that we got the document to which mom referred that had some details of her life. And those of you viewing this tape know much more about my mother factually, and probably a lot about her thoughts, than we know. We don't know these facts. We don't know the stories. And we don't know what's on this tape or what you've heard. We'll find out later. I think that what I'd like to comment about that is that it didn't matter. It just didn't matter. We grew up with the-- [SOBS] with the pain, and the emotion. The look in her eyes. The bad dreams. The things that were unsaid. And there were many, many, many things that were unsaid. And she didn't have to tell us what, what she went through. But I'm glad that you'll get to know it, and that we'll get to know more of it now. I'll be 46 years old, and I grew up without knowing about it. I don't know what her life was like as a child, or what the house was like, what my grandparents were like. I just don't know. And I think that's something that I do miss. And I told her that I would've missed that. I would've liked to have that more. But in terms of the influence on my life, on other people's lives, obviously I'm very emotional about it now. But I can honestly say that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm so proud of her, as we all are, that you don't have to know the horrible things about a person's past to see how much they've contributed, and to see the changes, literally, in the world that she can make through what she has done, in terms of giving, giving to people through the Holocaust Museum, through teachings, through her life as a human being, as a parent, as an educator. That's the thing we're most proud of. And that has come out of the depth of her experiences, the facts of which she hasn't shared with us. But we've learned from the other part. And now you have the benefit of both. You have the benefit of knowing what she went through, and the facts, and knowing her as a person. You, you have the benefit, and the chance, of taking all of this and bettering your life from that. And I hope you take advantage of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a, a different kind of childhood, growing up as we did in Miami, in the circumstances that you have learned today. Yet what-- one of the things that sticks out in my mind as the most influential on me and how I developed was in our, Judith's and mine younger years-- not too younger years, early teenage years-- when my mother and father decided, I suppose mutually, that-- for mom to resume her education and bring in an extra income for our future benefit. And this was at a time when we were-- as I said, our early teenage years. And mom went not only back-- I don't know how much she related to you in the-- in, in earlier tape, but she had to take a high school equivalency test in order to allow her to enter junior college, and then further going to get degrees at University of Miami. This is all while we were in school. So that during the years-- and this was during a time when it was really unheard of for a mother to be doing this kind of thing. In any case, her accomplishment in a very short period of time, which to us seemed totally natural and obvious because of her intellectual gifts-- it was extraordinary to see her finally come to flower and be able to put to general use what we knew personally from her, and which has now come to be quite a, a, a, a overwhelming career for her. And it, I suppose, taught me specifically, personally, that you can accomplish things personally. And the more we learn, and the more we get information from her, from before we knew her, before we were born, of course it's simply a reflection of that, that strength that she, she showed so courageously. And it's been a great source of strength for me. And at a time in my life where it's been my aim to be personally creative and personally satisfied, it is something that I look back upon and gain strength from. So I think, in terms of my life, that's been a great influence.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, Gary had just reminded me of something. And I don't know if Helen told you about it. But there was a time at the University of Miami when they nominate people for Woodrow Wilson scholarships. I don't know if she told you this.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in my bio, sweetheart.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in your bio?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in my bio, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what is not in your bio, which she always denies, as usual, because of her modesty, is that when they went for the interview-- I believe they went up to, to Orlando. And there was another young man who was nominated for the Woodrow Wilson scholarship in English, too. From what I heard, that they denied it for her because, quote, 'she was too old' to pursue an education for the rest of her life while the young man had many years ahead of her. As it turned out, they were 100% right. It was every year after that, when she became a professor at the University, she was constantly nominated as Professor of the Year. And she stayed there for 17 years. And in my opinion, if she wanted to, if she had been ambitious enough--

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't brag.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which she's not. Don't brag, see? She-- that's right. She could have been, easily, a Dean of the university.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sweetheart, you make me uncomfortable. Please don't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well because it's honest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Please don't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these are the examples that I'm telling you about, the standards that she set, the moral precepts that she sets. And I've always told her, and I confess it, that all that I have become because of these standards-- you look around the house, it's all here. From the books, and the libraries, and the-- and the photographs, and the things that are meaningful to us, including all the refrigerator and freezer that's constantly filled. But this is our life. And this is what she has brought to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's enough, sweetheart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like very much for you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And explain where it's from.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was at University of Miami, I was actually Associate Professor of English. And I introduced a course, literature of the Holocaust. I introduced this course simply because I was sort of made to confront it. And that happened, actually, through a coincidence. Elie Wiesel came to the university, and he gave a lecture, and I gave a reception for him in my house. And I asked the question, how he can confront it. And he says, if you and I don't do it, we will leave it to other people to use their fantasies, and their imagination.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I confronted it, and I offered a course on literature of the Holocaust, which became very, very successful. And because of that, I then offered another course, which was roots of antisemitism, because I realized that a number of students who came to my previous course didn't understand why the Holocaust would have ever happened. And before I knew, I was asked to organize a new program, which was program in Judaic Studies. And before I knew, I was asked to become Director of Judaic Studies. And I taught a number of courses on the Holocaust and on-- especially on the moral lessons of the Holocaust. It came to a point where some of my students petitioned the dean to make my course a compulsory and a prerequisite course to the teaching of philosophy, and morality, and ethics, because they felt that that's what I taught them. So when I retired, I was given a plaque. And the plaque actually is hanging right now at the library of the University of Miami. And unbeknownst to me, they took a quotation from something that I had said or written. And they put it on the plaque.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll confess to you, sweetheart. The quotation was given to them by me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that. This is the quotation. 'This I believe. As a survivor of the Holocaust, I represent to my students a reality of the Holocaust and a living proof that the experiences described in the literature of the Holocaust are based on fact, not on fiction. Dehumanization, genocide, planned extermination of innocent millions became a stunning manifestation of man's prejudice, unfounded hate, and ignorance. We must therefore attempt to examine our own prejudices by exploring the dehumanization of man, which resulted from the prejudices of other man. The Holocaust must serve as a model and become an example of the implementation of the potential man possesses for the destruction of other men.'

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Love you, mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Love you, sweetheart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Gary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Jewish mother, the pillar of my life. Strength and a guiding light, a towering spirit, and a loving friend. My mother was goodness, she was wisdom, she was kindness. She is no more. But goodness, wisdom, and kindness will always remain to me synonymous with my mother. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my mother. This is her engagement picture. The photograph was taken, obviously, prior to her wedding. The wedding took place in 1913. Her first name in Hebrew is [? Yohevet ?]. Her Polish name was Eva or Eva. Her last-- maiden name was Heiman, H-E-I-M-A-N. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photo was taken in Lodz, Poland, and the date of the photo was around 1913. [PAUSES FOR 57 SECONDS] This is a photo of my father as a yeshiva bocher. He was studying, very seriously, Talmud and Jewish studies. And that was the attire that, at that time, people who were scholars wore. My mother's photo is right after their marriage. Note that she's wearing a little wig because, at that time, after you got married, you were supposed not to show your hair. But she wore a wig, which she very soon removed and she kept her own hair and never wore a wig again. Those are my parents at the time of their marriage, very soon after their marriage. My father's name, Hebrew name, is Shlomo. English or Polish name was Salomon Neimark, N-E-I-M-A-R-K, which he alternately would change to Neumark, N-EU-M-A-R-K. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I imagine that the place for the photo would have been around [? Ustka, ?] Poland, where he-- they lived right after their marriage. The date of the photo may be, probably, about 1914. I would like to read a poem I wrote about my father. 'My father left me the memories of books, many books. Of reading [INAUDIBLE] and Herzl and Bialik, Spinoza and Heine, Baal Shem Tov, Sholem Aleichem, and even Nietzsche. He left the memories of the spirit of tradition, the sanctity of the Sabbath, the Megilllah reading at Purim, the solemnity of Yom Kippur, the joyful Simchat Torah, the serenity of a Passover Seder. He dressed in a shimmering white and gold kittel, melodiously chanting the Haggadah, the story of slavery, and the exodus. My father, he showed me the way.' [PAUSES FOR 126 SECONDS] This picture is of my mother visiting me and my older sister in Rabka, Poland. Rabka was a resort place in the Carpathian mountains, Tatra, in Poland. And we were there at a summer camp. And my mother and father would occasionally come to visit us on the way to their resort place which was Krynica, not far from Rabka. Here, my mother obviously visited us alone without my father. And we were dressed in our finery, in our white dresses, and white bows in our hair. The picture was taken, probably, around [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1928, or 1929, or 1930. I don't remember how old I may have been here. [PAUSES FOR 86 SECONDS] This picture is of my parents around 1937, 1938, just about a year or so before the beginning of World War II. And this is a poem that I wrote in memory of my parents. 'My parents taught me that there is a God, and that God is good and merciful. They taught me to believe in this God, for he gave us the law, the Torah, which we must uphold, for it is good and right for all men to be moral. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A man, especially a Jew who has been chosen by God to lead the way, must live by the law to deserve his fate and to be blessed by God. And my parents, they paid honor to God. They obeyed the law. And they paid honor to man. They respected their fellow man.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were good, and righteous, pious and humble. For didn't the prophet say, what does the Lord require of thee but to do justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with thy God? And God saw how the Nazis took my mother and father, how they led them to the ovens, and how they murdered them. Shouldn't I-- why shouldn't I at least have the right to ask why?'
